segments.s192-g1: This is a classical Persian 'X را Y بود' construction meaning 'X had Y' (the را here marks the possessor/topic rather than a direct object). فرود جوان is the possessor phrase ('Farud the young'), دژ آباد is a noun + adjective 'a prosperous/inhabited fortress', and بود is the past/copular verb 'was' completing the clause ('had'). Word order: possessor + را + possessed + بود.
segments.s192-g2: This short clause enumerates what was in the fortress. درپرستنده is a plural agent/occupant noun 'attendants/court‑worshippers', هفتاد is the numeral 'seventy', and بود is the past copula 'there were'. The first word (بدژ) appears here as a locative/relational element tied to 'fortress' in the previous segment; the clause as a whole states the number of attendants.
segments.s192-g3: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone' modifying the plural subject ماهرویان ('moon‑faced ones', i.e. beauties). بباره is a verbal stem meaning 'to pour/flow' used metaphorically, and بدند is a classical 3rd‑person plural verbal ending used here as the past/optative auxiliary ('they came/poured'). The phrase means 'all the moon‑faced ones poured forth.'
segments.s192-g4: چو is the conjunction 'when/as'. دیبای چینی is a noun phrase 'Chinese brocade/silk' (دیبا ‘brocade’, چینی 'Chinese' as adjective). نظاره is a noun 'a look/sight' used with the verbal auxiliary بدند to form the verbal idea 'they cast a look / they beheld'; here نظاره بدند = 'they looked/gazed'. The clause describes the act of looking at the Chinese brocade.
segments.s192-g5: ازان 'from that/thereupon' is a circumstantial connector; بازگشتن is the infinitive/masdar 'the returning' or a verbal concept 'to return'. فرود جوان is again the named person 'Farud the young'. Together the phrase sets the time/occasion: 'at/from that return of Farud the young' or 'when Farud the young returned.'
segments.s192-g6: از ایشان here functions causally/relationally ('on their part' / 'because of them' / 'from them'), همی is the classical particle indicating continued state ('still' / ongoing), بود is the past copula 'was', and تیره‌روان is an adjective meaning 'dark‑faced, gloomy'. The clause says that (they) were gloomy on that occasion.
segments.s192-g7: چنین is the adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here is the verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses are dialogic and do not match the simple speech verb in context), با is the preposition 'to/with', and شاهزاده تخوار is the addressee phrase 'the prince (takhvār / possibly an epithet 'wretched prince' or a proper name)'. The phrase introduces reported speech: 'Thus he said to the prince...'.
segments.s192-g8: که‌گر is a conditional particle equivalent to 'اگر' meaning 'if/when'. جست is from جستن and here means 'to seek' ('if you seek'). خواهی is the 2nd‑person modal 'you wish/will/intend' (here 'you desire to'), همی is the classical aspect/tense particle used with verbs for emphasis/continuity (often rendered 'indeed' or as part of the verbal phrase), and کارزار is the object 'battle/struggle'. Together they form the conditional admonition: 'If you wish to seek, you shall/should indeed take up battle.'
segments.s192-g17: This clause is Subject–Object–Verb (typical classical Persian order). فرود (here a proper name) is the subject, followed by an agentive/ablative از + تخوار meaning “from the wretch,” then the demonstrative این + plural سخنها “these words,” and the past tense verb شنید “heard.” شنید is a simple past (pre‑modern) form meaning ‘he heard’ or ‘he listened (to).’
segments.s192-g18: کمان را places the definite object (the bow) before the verb with the object marker را. بزه کرد is a short verbal phrase where کرد is the main past‑tense verb ‘did/made’ and بزه is what he did to the bow (an archaic/poetic verbal noun here; the exact dictionary sense in the supplied list doesn’t match the classical verbal use, so treat it as an action of manipulating the bow). و اندر کشید joins the next action with و “and”; اندر is an adverbial/locative particle here (‘in/into’ or ‘inward’) and کشید is the past of کشیدن “to pull/draw.”
segments.s192-g19: This is a verb‑final clause where the instrument or patient appears before the verb: خدنگی (a dagger) + بر اسپ (upon/against the steed) + سپهبد (the spahbed, title) and then the past verb بزد ‘he struck/struck at’. بر expresses spatial/target relation ‘against/on’ and بزد is the simple past of زدن ‘to strike.’
segments.s192-g20: چنان کز introduces a result/comparison clause: چنان ‘so/thus’ + کز (= که از/that) ‘that/from which’. The remainder, کمان سواران سزد, is an impersonal construction with سزد ‘it befits/it is fitting’ as predicate; کمان سواران is a genitival phrase ‘the bow of the horsemen’ (i.e. the horsemen’s bows). The verb سزد is present/optative‑like in classical Persian meaning ‘is fitting/appropriate.’
segments.s192-g21: This clause uses the past auxiliary/verb شد after a predicate: نگون شد ‘was overturned/felled’ — نگون here means ‘overthrown/downfallen’. سر تازی places the affected noun ‘the Arab’s head’ before شد. The coordinate و + جان بداد links the result ‘and (he) gave his life’: جان ‘life’ as object and بداد is an archaic/past form meaning ‘gave’ in context (the provided sense list does not include this verbal use, so it’s irregular relative to the supplied senses).
segments.s192-g22: This is a nominal/predicative string describing Tūs: دل طوس = ‘the heart of Tūs’ followed immediately by پرکین (a proper name or epithet) set as apposition or predicate; then و connects to سر پر ز باد ‘and (his) head full of wind.’ پر here is the adjectival particle ‘full (of),’ ز = ‘of/from,’ and باد is literal ‘wind.’ Word order is noun + modifier/apposition, typical in poetry where verbs are omitted when implied.
segments.s192-g23: بلشکر گه آمد is a temporal/occasional clause: بلشکر ‘with an army’ (or ‘a great host’) + گه ‘occasion/moment/sometimes’ + آمد ‘came’ (past). بگردن سپر is a prepositional/locative phrase literally ‘to/upon the neck of the shield’ — بگردن using the preposition بـ + گردن ‘neck’ and then سپر ‘shield’ as the possessor/object. The verb آمد governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s192-g24: This is a nominal description: پیاده (infantry/foot soldiers) is the head noun; پر از گرد means ‘full of dust’ (پر = ‘full (of)’, از = ‘of/from’, گرد = ‘dust’); و آسیمه سر adds a coordinate adjective phrase ‘and distraught/hurried of head’ where آسیمه سر is a compound adjective meaning ‘headlong, distraught, confused.’ Word order is noun + adjectival/participle modifiers, typical in descriptive lines.
segments.s192-g49: This clause is a compact, poetic statement: زرسپ (proper name) is followed by the epithet گرانمایه (postpositive adjective = “precious/esteemed”). ز (poetic contraction of از) + و here form زو meaning “from him/it.” شد is the past-tense verb “became/was,” and بباد is a verbal phrase lit. “to the wind” meaning “was blown away / was lost.” The whole string functions as a passive/result clause: “Zarasp the precious was blown away from him.”
segments.s192-g50: This is a nominal phrase identifying the agent or actor: سواری is a rider/horseman, سرافراز is a postpositive epithet meaning “triumphant/lofty,” and نوذرنژاد is a family/lineage name “descendant of Nowzar.” In the poem the agent follows the passive clause without an explicit preposition, so this names who caused the loss: “the triumphant rider, of Nowzar’s line.”
segments.s192-g51: بخونست here is a compact/poetic past expression (literally something like به خون نشست) meaning “fell into blood / was plunged into blood” (i.e., was bloodied or slain). غرقه is an adjective “drenched/immersed,” and تن is the noun “body.” ریونیز is a rare/uncertain word in the manuscript; here it functions as a qualifying adverb/epithet attached to the clause. The clause describes the corpse soaked in blood.
segments.s192-g52: ازین is the fused form of از + این (“from this / out of this”). بیش خواری = “greater/further humiliation” (بیش = more; خواری = humiliation/abasement). چه بینیم is a rhetorical interrogative: چه = “what” and بینیم is present-subjunctive/optative “shall we see / might we see.” نیز (= also/too) reinforces the question. The line asks rhetorically what additional disgrace remains to be seen after this.
segments.s192-g53: گرو پور جمست is a compact nominal phrase: گرو (here a name or group) + پور = “son/descendant” + جمست (name element, here the ancestor Jamest/Jam). The verb جمست in the provided forms can also mean “gathered,” but in this word order پور جمست indicates a patronymic (X, son of Jamest). و links this unit to مغز قباد, where مغز (literally “brain”) is used as epithet “the wise/counsel” and قباد is the name Qobad. Together the two coordinated noun phrases set the actors of the next verb.
segments.s192-g54: بنادانی here refers to the foundations/establishing (or the founders) and functions with برگشاد (past “opened, unveiled”) as the verbal idea. این جنگ را is the direct object (این = “this,” جنگ = “war/battle,” را = object marker). The clause means that (they) opened/revealed or set in motion the foundations/causes of this battle; برگشاد is a simple past (perfective) verb “opened / disclosed.”
segments.s192-g33: سپهبد (subject) + فرود آمد is a compound past tense verb meaning 'descended' or 'alighted' (literary). The following prepositional phrase از کوه سر = 'from the mountain's summit' places the origin; سر here means 'summit/top'. Word order is Subject – Verb – Prepositional phrase, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s192-g34: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they went/they departed' (verb-first order common in classical lines). گردان ('troop') is the group referred to; پر اندوه is an adjectival phrase 'full of sorrow' modifying (or describing) the troop, and سر ('head') here means 'head' — i.e. 'with sorrowful/downcast heads'. The whole phrase reads as 'the troop departed, heads full of sorrow.'
segments.s192-g35: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that / for that', اکنون 'now', تو بازآمدی is 2nd-person singular past 'you returned' (classical past form), and تندرست is a predicate adjective 'healthy/sound' placed after the verb. The clause means 'now that you have returned healthy.'
segments.s192-g36: بآب is the classical prepositional form 'with/ by water' (بِ + آب). مژه 'eyelash' is used metonymically for 'tears' (i.e. 'the water of the eyelash'). رخ is the direct object 'face'. نبایست is the older negative modal 'should not / ought not', and شست is an archaic/clausal verbal form of 'wash' (here functioning like an infinitive/past form to complete the modal). Together: 'one ought not to wash the face with the water of the eyelash' — i.e. 'do not wash your face with tears.'
segments.s192-g37: بپیچید is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'he turned aside / turned away' (the prefix بـ here is verbal and part of the classical verbal form). زان کار = 'from that matter' (زان = 'from that', کار = 'affair/matter'), and پرمایه 'substantial/important' qualifies the matter. The proper name گیو appears after the clause: classical narrative often places the verb before the subject, so this reads 'Giv turned away from that substantial matter.'
segments.s192-g38: که here links the following explanatory clause ('for/that'). آمد is past 'came'. پیاده functions adverbially 'on foot' (as opposed to mounted). سپهدار is a title 'commander' and نیو is a proper name attached to it (the name is not one of the provided dictionary senses). The sequence is essentially 'for the commander Niu came on foot.'
segments.s192-g39: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech or a statement. گفت is the classical past 'he said' (here used as the speech verb). کین را is the definite object 'this vengeance' (را marks the object). خود 'itself/indeed' is emphatic/reflexive, and اندازه نیست 'has no measure/limit' uses اندازه = 'measure/extent' with نیست = 'is not,' yielding 'it has no measure' i.e. 'it is beyond measure.'
segments.s192-g40: رخ نامداران = 'the face(s) of the renowned' (a genitive/possessive relation). برین is the fused prepositional phrase 'بر + این' = 'upon/among this' (classical form brīn = 'in/on this / among these'). تازه نیست is the idiom 'is not new' meaning 'this is not unprecedented' — overall: 'such a face among the famous is not new.'
segments.s192-g25: گواژه همی زد پس او فرود is built of a short clause + a verb of motion. گواژه (literally “witness/testimony”) is used here as a noun that the speaker utters or invokes; همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or emphatic action ("kept/was"); زد is the simple past of زدن used here in the sense of producing/sounding (to utter/cry). پس provides the sequence "then," and او فرود (pronoun + noun) uses فرود as a nominalized/elliptical word meaning "descent/alighting" — together: “he cried out and then alighted.”
segments.s192-g26: که این نامور پهلوان را چه بود is a subordinate/explanatory clause introduced by که (here linking and roughly meaning “for/that/asking”), with این (=“this”) + نامور پهلوان “renowned champion” as the topical/definite phrase (را marks it as definite/object/topic). چه بود is a past interrogative construction meaning “what was (the matter)?” or “what ailed (him)?” — i.e. a rhetorical question about the champion's condition.
segments.s192-g27: که ایدون ستوه آمد از یک سوار opens with که linking this remark to the previous sentence. ایدون is an older/formal word meaning “until now / came” (here sense of ‘came/arrived’); ستوه is an adjective meaning “weary/distressed”; آمد is the past tense verb “came.” از یک سوار literally “from one rider” indicates the origin/agent: “a single rider came (and he was) weary.”
segments.s192-g28: چگونه چمد در صف کارزار is a short verbless clause introduced by چگونه (“how”), setting up a simile or wonder. چمد (here taken metaphorically) is treated like “bolt/latch” (a sudden projectile image); در صف کارزار places it spatially: در = “in,” صف = “line/formation” (here the battle-line), کارزار = “battle.” Together it reads as an exclamatory simile: “how (he appeared) like a bolt in the battle-line.”
segments.s192-g29: پرستندگان خنده برداشتند is a simple past clause: پرستندگان (“worshippers/devotees” — here attendants or adherents) is the subject, خنده (“laughter”) is the object/what they take, and برداشتند is the past plural of برداشتن used idiomatically as “they raised/broke into” (i.e., they burst into laughter). The word order is Subject–Object–Verb, standard for Persian.
segments.s192-g30: همی از چرم نعره برداشتند continues the action: همی (progressive/emphatic particle) gives an ongoing/instantaneous sense (“then/thereupon”), از marks source (“from”), چرم literally “leather” (here metonymically ‘leather armor’), نعره “a shout/cry,” and برداشتند again = “they raised” or “they let out.” So: “then from their leather (armor) they raised a cry.”
segments.s192-g31: که پیش جوانی یکی مرد پیر begins with که (linking). پیش جوانی = “in front of/before the young man” (prepositional phrase with پیش), یکی مرد پیر = “one/ a certain old man” (یکی = a certain/one, مرد = man, پیر = old). Word order is topical fronting of the location/person followed by the identifying phrase.
segments.s192-g32: ز افراز غلتان شد از بیم تیر is the event clause: ز (poetic form of از) = “from,” افراز = “the heights/lofty place,” غلتان is the present participle/adjective “rolling” (describing the manner), شد is past of شدن used here as a change-of-state verb “became/was made” (so: “rolled”), then از = “from/because of,” بیم = “fear,” تیر = “arrow.” Together: “he rolled down from the heights, from fear of an arrow.”
segments.s192-g41: اگر begins a conditional clause 'if'. شهریارست is an archaic fused form (شهریار + است) meaning 'he/she/it is king' (third-person copula fused as -ست); با + گوشوار is a prepositional phrase 'with an earring/ornament' that modifies شهریارست. Word order is compacted and poetic: copula-suffixed predicate + instrument phrase.
segments.s192-g42: چه here is an interrogative 'what'. گیرد is a present/irrealis 3rd-person form of گرفتن 'to take, get' (verb precedes its subject in this poetic line). چنین modifies the following noun 'لشکر' = 'such a/so great', while گشن (hungry) and خوار (despicable/worthless) are adjectival modifiers of لشکر. The clause asks rhetorically what such an army will obtain.
segments.s192-g43: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / we should not'. که introduces the subordinate clause. باشیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present 'we be' (here optative/permission sense 'let us not be'); همداستان is the predicate meaning 'in agreement / accomplice'. Together: 'We should not be like-minded/accomplices.'
segments.s192-g44: به هر گونه‌ای is an adverbial phrase 'in every/any manner' (به + گونه‌ای makes an adverbial 'in the manner of ...'). کو زند داستان is a relative/archaic clause 'who/which tells the story' (داستان is 'story'); here کو and زند function as the relative/verb pair 'who tells' (archaic usage). The whole phrase qualifies the kind of agreement being rejected: 'in whatever way the tale is told.'
segments.s192-g45: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. طوس here is the personal name Tūs (the hero). یک‌بار is an adverb 'once'. تندی is a noun meaning 'fierceness/anger' (an adverbial/nominal complement), and نمود is the past/formal past of 'to show' (نمود = 'showed'). The clause means 'If Tūs once showed fierceness.'
segments.s192-g46: زمانه is 'the age' or 'the times' (subject). پرآزار is a predicate adjective 'full of harm/troublesome'. گشت is the past of گشتن used impersonally/causatively 'became'. از + فرود is a prepositional/causal phrase 'because of / from the fall/descent' (از indicating cause). The sequence yields 'the times became very troublesome because of the fall.'
segments.s192-g47: همه جان فدای سیاوش کنیم is an optative/volitive exhortation: همه جان 'all lives' (accusative/goal) + فدای (short for فدای کردن) 'sacrifice for' + سیاوش (object). کنيم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/imperative 'let us do' — together 'let us all give our lives for Siavash.' The object (همه جان) precedes the verb in typical Persian order.
segments.s192-g48: نباید is the impersonal modal 'we must not / it should not be that'. که introduces the clause. این 'this' points to the foregoing matter; بد modifies فرامش (an older form meaning 'forgetfulness/neglect'), and کنیم is 1st-plural subjunctive 'we do' — 'we must not commit this bad forgetfulness' (i.e. we must not forget/neglect this).
segments.s192-g9: "نگر" is an imperative/interjection meaning "look" or "consider." The noun phrase "نامور طوس را" places the adjective "نامور" (renowned) before the name "طوس" and uses the object/topic marker "را" to mark Tous as the target; "نشکنی" is an older negative present/subjunctive (negated stem + -ی) addressed to the second person, used predictively here: "you will not defeat/break (him)."
segments.s192-g10: "ترا" (written here split as ت + را) is the clitic second‑person object/dative meaning "to you"; the phrase "آن به آید" literally "that will come" is an idiom meaning "that will befall/ happen to (you)." "که" introduces the content clause, and "اسپ افگنی" is a compact verb phrase: "اسپ" (steed) + the archaic verb form "افگنی" (from افگن/افگندن) meaning to cast/throw off, i.e. "you will be unhorsed." The whole clause predicts this result for the addressee.
segments.s192-g11: "و دیگر" = "and besides/moreover." The subordinate marker "که باشد" here introduces a hypothetical/optative sense ("if there should be"); "مر" = dative/for-me ("for me"), "او را" = "him" (object/topic-marked), and "زمان" = "time/opportunity" — together: "if there should be for me an opportunity against him."
segments.s192-g12: "نیاید" is a negative-present/optative form meaning "will not come/appear." The prepositional phrase "به یک چوبه تیر از کمان" is literal: "by/ even a single shaft (چوبه) of an arrow (تیر) from the bow (از کمان)." The clause means no single arrow-shaft will come from the bow — i.e. not a single arrow will be sent.
segments.s192-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. آمد is a 3rd-person past (here narrative) verb 'came/reached' and appears before its subject سپهبد (verb–subject order is common in epic poetry). بر + noun expresses motion onto/upon, so بر این تیغ کوه means 'upon this tiġ (metaphorical 'ridge/point') of the mountain' — تیغ used poetically for a sharp mountain-crest.
segments.s192-g14: بیاید is a 3rd-person form used in narrative (historic or optative feel) meaning 'comes/arrives' (here rendered as simple present/narrative). کنون 'now' is an adverb modifying the clause. لشکرش is the noun 'laskar' (army, host) plus the possessive suffix -ش 'his' (the subject follows the verb). همگروه qualifies the army — 'together/all in company' — so the clause means his whole host now comes together.
segments.s192-g15: ترا (ت + را) is the 2nd-person object/benefactive form 'to/for you' (here functioning like 'you have/you are left without'); نیست is the existential/negative 'is not / there is not'. در جنگ 'in battle' is the locative phrase. پایاب اوی is a possessive phrase where پایاب (here used metaphorically as 'standing/foothold/place of safety' in the battle) takes the pronoun اوی 'his' — literally 'his پایاب'. The clause therefore means 'You have no پایاب (no standing/secure place) in battle with him.'
segments.s192-g16: ندیدی is the past negative (2nd-person) 'you did not see' (often read as 'you have not seen'). بروهای is a plural/collective form from برو (here functioning as 'those that go/fly' — i.e. projectiles that fly), modified by پرتاب 'throwing/launching' to mean 'the things launched/thrown' and اوی is the genitive 'his'. Word order places the negative past verb before its object phrase in the poetic register: 'You have not seen his thrown (projectiles/volley).'

sentences.s192-s1: Farud the young had a prosperous fortress; in that fortress there were seventy attendants.
sentences.s192-s2: All the moon‑faced ones poured forth; when they looked, they gazed at Chinese brocade.
sentences.s192-s3: When Farud the young returned from that, they (on their part) were dark‑faced (gloomy).
sentences.s192-s4: Thus he said to the prince (or to the wretched prince), 'If you seek (to do so), you should indeed take up the battle.'
sentences.s192-s9: Farud heard these words from the wretch, bent his bow and drew it.
sentences.s192-s10: He struck the spahbed’s steed with a dagger, in such a manner that it suits the bowmen.
sentences.s192-s11: The Arab’s head was overthrown and he gave up his life; Tūs’s heart (was) Perkin, and his head was full of wind.
sentences.s192-s12: The host/army came (at a time) with many soldiers upon the shield’s neck; the infantry, covered with dust and distraught, (were ...).
sentences.s192-s25: Zarasp, the precious one, was blown away from him by the triumphant rider, descendant of Nowzar.
sentences.s192-s26: His body sank, drenched in blood; from this greater humiliation what shall we (yet) see?
sentences.s192-s27: Garu, son of Jamest, gathered, and the wise Qobad opened (laid bare) the foundations of this war.
sentences.s192-s17: The spahbed descended from the mountain's summit; the troop departed, their heads full of sorrow.
sentences.s192-s18: For now you have returned sound and healthy; one must not wash the face with the water of the eyelash.
sentences.s192-s19: Giv turned away from that important matter, for the commander Niu came on foot.
sentences.s192-s20: Thus he said: 'This vengeance has no measure in itself; such a face among the renowned is not a new thing.'
sentences.s192-s13: Then he dismounted, crying out, "What ailed this famed champion?"
sentences.s192-s14: For until then a single weary rider had come — how like a bolt in the battle-line.
sentences.s192-s15: The devotees burst into laughter; then from their leather (armor) they raised a cry.
sentences.s192-s16: Before the youth, one old man rolled down from the heights, trembling for fear of an arrow.
sentences.s192-s21: If he is a king with an earring, what will such a hungry, despicable host gain?
sentences.s192-s22: We ought not to be accomplices, in whatever way someone tells the tale.
sentences.s192-s23: If Tūs once showed anger/fierceness, the age became full of trouble because of the downfall.
sentences.s192-s24: Let us all sacrifice our lives for Siavash; we must not allow this bad forgetfulness.
sentences.s192-s5: Look — you will not defeat the renowned Tous; it will befall you that you will be unhorsed.
sentences.s192-s6: And moreover, if I should have an opportunity against him, not a single shaft of an arrow will come from the bow.
sentences.s192-s7: When the spahbed reached the ridge of this mountain, now his whole host came together.
sentences.s192-s8: You have no standing in battle against him; you have not seen the flights of his thrown projectiles.
